coredata.dc:title: Transparent sunlight conversion film based on carboxymethyl cellulose and carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: You, Yaqin
Zhang, Haoran
Liu, Yingliang
Lei, Bingfu
coredata.dc:description: Transparent sunlight conversion film based on carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and carbon dots (CDs) has been developed for the first time through dispersion of CDs in CMC aqueous solution. Due to the hydrogen bonds interaction, CMC can effectively absorb the CDs, whose surfaces are functionalized by lots of polar groups. The results from atomic force microscopy (AFM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) confirm that the composite film possesses a homogeneous and compact structure. Besides, the CMC matrix neither competes for absorbing excitation light nor absorbs the emissions of CDs, which reserves the inherent optical properties of the individual CDs. The composite films can efficiently convert ultraviolet light to blue light. What’s more, the film is transparent and possesses excellent mechanical properties, expected to apply in the field of agricultural planting for sunlight conversion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carboxymethyl cellulose
Carbon dots
Composite
Sunlight conversion
Film

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant Nos. 21571067 , 21371062 ), the Foundation for High-level Talents in Higher Education of Guangdong Province , the Project for Construction of High-level University in Guangdong Province, the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. S2013030012842 ), and the Provincial Science and technology project of Guangdong Province (Nos. 2014A010105035 , 2015B090903074 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM images of free surface for CMC film (a) and CMC/CDs film.
SEM images of cross-section for CMC film (a) and CMC/CDs film (b).
FT-IR spectra of carbon dots, CMC film and CMC/CDs film.
Stress-strain curve of CMC and CMC/CDs films.

Optical characterization of the composite films. (a) Absorption spectra of CMC film, CDs, and CMC/CDs film, (b) Fluorescence spectrum and (c) the sunlight conversion effect of the CMC/CDs film, (d) Transmittance spectra of the CMC/CDs composite film with the concentration of CDs.

head.ce:title: Transparent sunlight conversion film based on carboxymethyl cellulose and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transparent sunlight conversion film based on carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and carbon dots (CDs) has been developed for the first time through dispersion of CDs in CMC aqueous solution. Due to the hydrogen bonds interaction, CMC can effectively absorb the CDs, whose surfaces are functionalized by lots of polar groups. The results from atomic force microscopy (AFM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) confirm that the composite film possesses a homogeneous and compact structure. Besides, the CMC matrix neither competes for absorbing excitation light nor absorbs the emissions of CDs, which reserves the inherent optical properties of the individual CDs. The composite films can efficiently convert ultraviolet light to blue light. What’s more, the film is transparent and possesses excellent mechanical properties, expected to apply in the field of agricultural planting for sunlight conversion.

ce:section.ce:para: Sunlight conversion film is a kind of functional film to change the wavelength of light, which shows great potential application in the field of agriculture. Based on plant physiology, the strongest absorption bands for the absorption of plant is located in blue light (400–500nm) and red light (600–680nm), motivating the synthesis of chlorophyll; while ultraviolet light (below 400nm) goes against, affecting the normal growth of plants and inducing disease from insect pests (Poudel, Kataoka & Mochioka, 2008; Kadur et al., 2007). Especially, like tomato (Xu et al., 2012) and cabbage (Kim et al., 2015), they show strong demand for blue light. Therefore, it is significant that sunlight conversion film is improved to convert the ultraviolet light into blue light (400–500nm). As we all know, sunlight conversion film is made up of two parts, including matrix and sunlight conversion agent, in which the latter plays a critical role in the performance of sunlight conversion properties. For sunlight conversion agent, many kinds of phosphors have been reported such as CaS:Cu+ (Zhang, Li, Chen, Xiao, & Sun 2004), CaAl2O4:Eu2+ (Li & Hintzen, 2006) and Ba3MgSi2O8:Eu2+ (He et al., 2009). Compared with these doped phosphor, carbon dots (CDs), which consists a fascinating class of fluorescence-based nanoparticles, have emerged as important fluorescence materials because of their excellent photostability, chemical stability, high biocompatibility, and cost-effective preparation (Qu, Wang, Lu, Liu, & Wang, 2012). Until now, CDs have gained increasing researchers’ attention and a great number of CDs are available for their promising candidates in sensing (Qu, Chen, Zheng, Cao, & Liu, 2013), bioimaging (Bhunia, Saha, Maity, Ray, & Jana, 2013), catalysis (Zhang, Yuan, Liang, &Yu, 2015), and energy conversion/storage devices (Li et al., 2011). Except for the properties above, as for fluorescence material, CDs can effectively absorb certain characteristic wavelength, and the electronic transitions from the ground state to the excited state. On other hand, a part of energy is released in the form of a photon when transition from the lowest excited state to the ground state. Thus, the whole process of sunlight conversion is completed. However, there is no report on the sunlight conversion properties of the carbon dots.

As we all know, environmental concern is always a focus of the world, especially towards the unreasonable utilization of fossil fuels (Bilgen, Kaygusuz & Sari, 2004). Therefore, more and more attention is paid to search for alternatives to replace the non-renewable sources (Khan, Huq, Khan, Riedl, & Lacroix, 2014). And biodegradable natural polymer including cellulose (Dar, Garai, Das, & Ghosh, 2010; Burman, Ghosh, &Das, 2014; Ghosh & Moulik, 1998; Nag, Sadhukhan & Chattoraj 1988; Qi, Chang, & Zhang, 2009), starch (Dintcheva, La Mantia, & Arrigo, 2014), and chitin (Srinivasa & Tharanathan, 2007) is provided a broader development space. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), the most widely used cellulose derivative, composed of β-d-glucose and β-d-glucopyranosyl-2-O-(carboxymethyl)-monosodium salt connected via β-(1,4-glycosidic) bonds (Fig. 1 ), which is prepared through the reaction between a cellulose alkali with sodium monochloroacetate (Paunonen, 2013). Due to its non-toxicity, biocompatibility, biodegradability, and hydrophilicity, CMC has been gained considerably public interest (Das, Mondal, & Ghosh, 2016; Chakraborty, Chakraborty & Ghosh, 2006; Wang & Su, 2014). Especially, the CMC film possesses high transparency in the spectral region and excellent film forming ability (Luna Martínez et al., 2011). Recently, a lot of interest has been paid in the synthesis of hybrid nanocomposites based on CMC with potential application. On one hand, the mechanical strength properties of film are improved effectively with the addition of graphene oxide in sodium carboxymethyl cellulose as high performance film (Yadav, Rhee, Jung, & Park, 2013). On the other hand, optical properties have been discussed in the luminescent films through situ precipitation of ZnS in sodium carboxymethyl cellulose aqueous solution (Luna Martínez et al., 2011). But the sunlight conversion properties of the composite film have scarcely been discussed.

It is known that CMC is a kind of hydrophilicity polymer, containing large number of hydroxyl groups. Besides, the CDs are biocompatible, whose surfaces are functionalized by lots of polar groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and amide groups. So it is promising to integrate CDs with CMC through simple hybrid process. In this work, we convey an effective route to prepare sunlight conversion film through combining the CDs with the CMC matrix. The preparation process can be divided into two parts, including synthesize of CDs and fabrication of film. Composite film was obtained by casting after the CDs dispersed in CMC aqueous solution. This process is demonstrated to be a “green” system to produce sunlight conversion films, without toxic vapor and fluid produced. In addition, from the perspectives of economic, compared to rare earth irons doped phosphors for sunlight conversion, the entail original materials is rather cost-effective involving in the synthesis of CDs, which can be produced in a large scale. Following, the obtained film was characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to evaluate its structure. Besides, optical properties were analyzed by using UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The composite film presents optical transmission about 90% at 800nm influenced by the amount of CDs in the composite films. Furthermore, the sunlight conversion effect of the composite film is analyzed through fluorescence spectrometer connecting an extra Xe lamp. In addition, the mechanical properties of the film are discussed.

Carboxymethyl cellulose (viscosity: 800–1200mPas; DS=0.7) and citric acid was acquired from Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Besides, the weight-average molecular weight (Mw) of CMC was determined in an aqueous solution by laser light scattering to be 12.0×104. Ethylenediamine was supplied by commercial sources in China, and was of analytical grade. All reagents were used as received without additional purification.

Carbon dots were prepared according to the literature (Zhu et al., 2013): firstly, citric acid (1.26g) and ethylenediamine (1.61ml) was dissolved in deionized water (30ml). And then the solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (50ml) and heated at 200°C for 5h. After the reaction, the reactors were cooled to room temperature naturally. The product, which was yellow-brown and transparent, was separated by centrifugation and purified by dialysis. Finally, through vacuum distillation and desiccation, the brownish black solid was obtained. In order to weigh accurately, 10mg of CDs was dispersed in 10ml of deionized water for further steps.

In a typical process, 0.8g of CMC was dissolved in 50ml of deionized water at 60°C until a transparent solution was formed. After added 200μl of CDs solution (0.2mg of CDs), the mixture solution was constantly stirred for 1h at 60°C. And then the resultant solution was subjected to centrifugation at 166.66Hz (10000rpm) for 10min under ambient conditions for degasification. Finally, the composite solution was rapidly casted into a culture dish with side-to-side vibrations to make the solution well-distributed, and dried at 40°C under vacuum for 24h.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a FEI-Tecnai-12 transmission electron microscope. The sample was diluted with ethanol, and then dropped to the copper grid for observation. The surface morphology of the films was analyzed by an atomic force microscope (AFM) (Dimension Edge, America). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) observation was carried out with a MERLIN −Compact-FSEM microscope (Germany). For charactering the internal fine structure of the film, the cross section was prepared as follows. Firstly, the film was cut into rectangular strips in size of 0.5×2cm. And then the cross section was obtained after the rectangular strips immersed in a container full with liquid nitrogen. Secondly, for available to test, both sides of the film are pasted on the glass strip through the conductive adhesive. Following, with the help of conductive liquid, the cross section of the film was vertically fixed on the copper table. At last, the cross section of the dried films were sputtered with gold and characterized with 3kV accelerating voltage.

Thickness of the film was measured by a thickness gauge, and each value was expressed as an average of five measurements. The thickness of the film has been controlled to be about 40μm. Relative errors in thickness of each film were within ±2μm. Tensile tests were performed on a universal tensile tester (UTM 4204, Shenzhen SANS test machine, China) according to GB/T 1040.3-2006, equipped with a 200N load cell. Rectangular strips 1.2×15cm in size were cut from the films and tested with a span length of 10cm at a rate of 5.0mmmin−1.

Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) of the CDs in KBr discs were recorded with the region of 4000–400cm−1 on a FT-IR spectrometer (Vertex 70, Germany). Absorption spectra were measured with a UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu UV 2550A, Japan) at a wavelength from 200nm to 600nm. The excitation spectra and emission spectra of the films were operated on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). The sunlight conversion effect of the films was tested by equipped the fluorescence spectrophotometer with an extra 100W Xe lamp as a light source.

To fabricate the sunlight conversion film, the CDs was synthesized in advance. The obtained CMC/CDs films are transparent, and emit bright blue light under UV excitation. From the details above, it indicates that the sunlight conversion films are prepared successfully. Besides, for this system, CMC, as a matrix, is biodegradable and environmental friendly. What’s more, it is noted that aqueous solution is acted as a solvent, and there is no toxic evaporation generated during preparation of the film. Therefore, this process is an effective and environmentally friendly way to prepare sunlight conversion film, which is in accordance with the requirement of production on a large scale.

It is known to us that carbon dots offer quasi-spherical graphitic nanoparticles with mean diameter below 10nm (Wang et al., 2015). Thus, the TEM observations of CDs were performed, shown in Fig. 2 . As seen, the CDs are a spherical shape with homogeneous distribution, and the average size of CDs is about 4nm, which makes it a good candidate for doping additive. Furthermore, the lattice fringe is observed and lattice distance is calculated to be about 0.21nm, which are consistent with the (102) lattice planes of graphitic carbon (You, Zhang, Liu, & Lei 2016). For fluorescence material, high photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) is a primary property, which relates directly to their applications. Here, quinine sulphate (0.1mol/l H2SO4 as solvent; QY=0.54) is chosen as standards, and the more details see the reference (You et al., 2016). From the calculation, the QY of the CDs solution is about 80.4% in water, showing potential application for sunlight conversion.

To investigate the morphologies of the composite film, the typical AFM images of the CMC film and the CMC/CDs film are shown in Fig. 3 . From the results, the free surface of pure CMC film (Fig. 3a) possesses a compact and homogeneous structure, but after the CDs embedded in CMC matrix, the distant among the CMC molecules enlarges, which indicates the strong interaction formed between the CDs and CMC molecules in Fig. 3b. In addition, roughness, as a critical indicator, has great influence on the properties such as optical and mechanics properties. And in generally, two forms of roughness are recommended: root mean square roughness (RMS) and average roughness. However, the root mean square roughness can more accurately reflect the comprehensive situation of roughness. Therefore, the RMS of the film is analyzed. For pure CMC film, the value of RMS is smaller than other kinds of film, about 1.24nm, which suggest that the surface of the film is smooth. After added the CDs solution, the value of RMS for the CMC/CDs slightly increases, about 3.88nm. This result is corresponding to the literature (Zeng & Yan, 2015), which can be explained by the addition of CDs nanoparticle enlarge the distance between the CMC molecule, lending to reduce the fluidity of the solution. Besides, it confirms that there are strong interaction between the CDs and CMC molecule.

As for membranous material, the cross section of composite membranes is very important. Thus, the cross section of the film was presented to character the internal fine structure. The SEM images of CMC and CMC/CDs film is shown in Fig. 4 a and b, respectively. Clearly, the thickness of the CMC film and CMC/CDs film is about 42μm, which is consistent with the result from thickness gauge. From the result, the CMC film is presented smooth. But after mixed with CDs solution, the CMC/CDs film preserves a homogeneous and compact structure, which further suggests the strong interaction formed between CDs and CMC molecules.

Fig. 5 shows the FT-IR spectra of CDs, CMC film and CMC/CDs film. As seen in the spectrum of CDs, the peaks at 1315, 1560, 1660, 3371cm−1 can be attributed to CO, NH, CO, OH vibration bonds, respectively. Obviously, the abundant polar functional groups make CDs strongly hydrophilic, lead to the good dispersion in CMC aqueous solution. From the FT-IR spectra of CMC, it shows a broad absorption band at 3356cm−1, due to the stretching frequency of the −OH group. The presence of a strong absorption band at 1587cm−1 confirms the presence of COO group. The bands at 1415 and 1055cm−1 are assigned to −CH2 scissoring and COC stretching vibration, respectively. Besides, compared to the CDs, the spectra of CMC and hybrid film present a small peak located at 1260cm−1, which is caused by the vibration band of CO. But there is no absorption at 1260cm−1 in the spectrum of CDs, so this peak is a suitable for comparison. Following, the relative intensity of the OH, COO, CH2 and COC have been calculated. From the result, the ratio intensity of I (OH)/I(CO), I(COO)/I(CO), (CH2)/I(CO) and I(COC)/I(CO) is 7%, 43%, 42%, 52% higher than that of CMC film, respectively. According to the literature (Terrabuio, Richter, Silva, Brunsb, & Haiduke, 2014), it is noted that the intensity of infrared vibration band is determined by two factors, dipole moment and energy transition, respectively. However, the probability of energy transition is dependent on the properties of functional groups. As for a specific functional group, the intensity of infrared absorption band is relative to hydrogen bonds, conjugate groups, Fermi resonance and solvent effect (Galimberti et al., 2013). Besides, concentration of functional groups also has an influence on the intensity of vibration bands. Here, we use the ratio intensity for calculation. Therefore, the effect of concentration can be neglected. In addition, compared to CMC film, the spectrum of the hybrid film shows the same absorption peak which implies that the possible of the conjugate group and Femi resonance can be excluded. And, for this system, water is acted as solvent both in the preparation of CMC and hybrid film, so it is not possible that solvent effect leads to increase of the intensity. Thus, through the analysis above, the increasing of intensity can be attributed to the hydrogen bands, and indicates that there should be good miscibility between CMC and CDs (Yadav et al., 2013).

For a type of practical film, mechanical properties are the most crucial properties for its application. Therefore, the tensile test of the composite films was performed by a universal test device. For pure CMC film, the values of tensile strength reported in literature are different on a degree due to the differences of source, viscosity and the experimental conditions (Arinaitwe & Pawlik, 2014). In our study, Fig. 6 illustrates the typical stress-strain curves of CMC film and the CMC/CDs composite film. As shown, stress increases almost linearly with strain at an early stage, with nonlinear behavior occurring before maximum stress is reached for both composite film and CMC film. From the results, the tensile strength at break (σb) of the CMC/CDs is much higher than that of CMC film, whereas their elongation at break (εb) changes slightly. Moreover, the CMC film and CMC/CDs film exhibit excellent mechanical properties, about 31MPa, 48MPa, respectively. In other words, the tensile strength of the CMC/CDs film is 55% higher than that of the CMC film. This suggests that the addition of CDs can effectively improve the mechanical strength of the film. Meanwhile, the results support the present of strong interaction between CMC and the CDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of pure CMC film, CDs in water, and CMC/CDs film were recorded as shown in Fig. 7 a. It is notes that the pure CMC film has almost no absorption above 240nm; while the CMC/CDs film has continuous broad absorption in the ultraviolet region ranging from 240 to 420nm, especially a strong broad peak located in 360nm, similar to the absorption spectrum of CDs in water, which indicates the good combination of both the CDs and CMC in the composites. From the comparison, it is concluded that the optical properties of CDs have no obviously changes after embedded in the CMC matrix, which means that the CMC matrix neither competes for absorbing excitation light nor absorbs the emissions of CDs (Sun et al., 2014), and explains that the CMC can act as a suitable matrix for sunlight conversion.

It is well known that there are two peaks in the CDs of an excitation spectrum. The one, peaked at about 240nm can be ascribed to the π–π* transitions of the aromatic CC sp2 domains, which cannot produce observed fluorescence signal (Eda et al., 2010). The other, peaked at about 360nm is from the trapping of excited state energy of the surface states, which can induce to strong fluorescence (Qiao et al., 2010). Accordingly, the excitation spectrum of CMC/CDs film presents a peak at 356nm, which is consistent with the absorption spectrum, shown in Fig. 7b. In addition, the emission spectrum of CMC/CDs film is measured at the strongest excitation wavelength. From the results, the broad emission spectrum is ranging from 380 to 640nm, and the peak is located in 434nm. In other words, the composite film shows ultraviolet excitation and blue emitting, which implies it for the potential application as sunlight conversion film.

To further investigate the sunlight conversion effect of the CMC/CDs film better, sunlight was imitated by a 100W Xe lamp as light source. For comparison, the spectrum of the Xe light was recorded from 300 to 800nm. Next, after the light passes through the CMC/CDs film vertically, the transmission spectrum was captured at the same condition. Fig. 7c shows the sunlight conversion effect of the CMC/CDs film. From it, on one hand, the ultraviolet light (below 400nm) decreases significantly because of the CDs strongly absorption; on the other hand, in the left above of Fig. 7c, the blue light (400–500nm) develops a tendency to go up compared to the light source. Through the illustration above, a conclusion is drawn that the CMC/CDs film can efficiently convert ultraviolet light to blue light, providing the ideal sunlight conversion properties.

As is known to all, for sunlight conversion materials, the film must possess sunlight conversion properties. Above, we have confirmed that the film can effectively absorb ultraviolet light, and then convert it into blue light. What’s more, the film is required excellent optical transmission to ensure plant’s absorption of visible light. Therefore, the transmittance of the film is demanded to be higher than 85% at 800nm. Fig. 7d shows the transmittance spectra of the CMC/CDs films with the different concentration of CDs in the 200–800nm wavelength regions. In the spectrum of pure CMC film, the optical transmission is approximately 90% at 800nm. However, after added the CDs nanoparticles in the CMC matrix, the optical transmission is slightly decreased, but the value is also above 85% in the visible light region. This result is in accordance with the sodium carboxymethyl cellulose/cotton linter cellulose composite film (Oun & Rhim, 2015), indicating that CDs is compatible with the matrix and it does not hinder the passage of light through the film, which makes its good candidates as a transparent fluorescence film for sunlight conversion.

In conclusion, we have provided a universal technique to prepare transparent sunlight conversion film through effective dispersion of CDs with CMC solution. The CMC molecule contains large numbers of hydroxyl groups around the surfaces, which can effectively absorb the CDs, whose surfaces are functionalized by lots of hydroxyl, carboxyl and amide groups owing to the interaction of hydrogen bonds. The results reveal that the composite films exhibit structural homogeneity, excellent optical properties, and good tensile strength. Furthermore, the composite films can efficiently convert ultraviolet light into blue light due to the inherent optical properties of the individual CDs. This work provides an effective method to prepare the sunlight conversion film. And the process is environmental friendly, which is expected to apply in the field of agricultural planting.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant Nos. 21571067, 21371062), the Foundation for High-level Talents in Higher Education of Guangdong Province, the Project for Construction of High-level University in Guangdong Province, the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. S2013030012842), and the Provincial Science and technology project of Guangdong Province (Nos. 2014A010105035, 2015B090903074).
